revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database]
== See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]

{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.

{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]

revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:return the see also]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]

revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also! == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]

revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 6]] to [[Category:qq]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Tier 6]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]

revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]

{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]

{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|title=Transition of Administrative Divisions|years= - 1897}} {{s-ttl|title=Toichi District}} {{s-aft|after=[[Shiki District}} {{end}} {{Kinai Five Provinces Districts}}

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Toichi District]]
revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]

=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.

{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe

{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|title=Transition of Administrative Divisions|years= - 1897}} {{s-ttl|title=Toichi District}} {{s-aft|after=[[Shiki District}} {{end}} {{Kinai Five Provinces Districts}}
{{translated page|ja|十市郡|version=102374891|comment=Imported full ja history}}

}}

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Defunct districts of Nara Prefecture
revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[district]] in [[Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[:en:1880|1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[bakufu]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[Bakufu]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|title=Transition of Administrative Divisions|years= - 1897}} {{s-ttl|title=Toichi District}} {{s-aft|after=[[Shiki District}} {{end}} {{Kinai Five Provinces Districts}}
{{translated page|ja|十市郡|version=102374891|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[:en:1880|1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:大和国の郡]] into [[Category:Districts of Yamato Province]] via bot
revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[:en:1880|1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[:en:Kashihara|Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[Tawaramoto]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[:en:Keiō|Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|title=Transition of Administrative Divisions|years= - 1897}} {{s-ttl|title=Toichi District}} {{s-aft|after=[[Shiki District}} {{end}} {{Kinai Five Provinces Districts}}
{{translated page|ja|十市郡|version=102374891|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 6]]
[[Category:History of Kashihara, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sakurai, Nara]]
}}
[[sn:Toichi district]] [[ja:十市郡]]

revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[:en:1880|1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[:en:Kashihara|Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[:en:Sakurai|Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[Shiki District]], [[:en:Tawaramoto, Nara|Tawaramoto, Nara]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following [[Shikinaisha]]: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[:en:Keiō|Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[:en:Uda District|Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:磯城郡]] into [[Category:Shiki District]] via bot
revision.text:
{{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[:en:1880|1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[:en:Kashihara|Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[:en:Sakurai|Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[:en:Shiki District|Shiki District]], [[:en:Tawaramoto, Nara|Tawaramoto, Nara]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|lt=Shikinaisha}}: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[:en:Keiō|Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[:en:Shiki District|Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[:en:Uda District|Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[:en:Shiki District|Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|title=Transition of Administrative Divisions|years= - 1897}} {{s-ttl|title=Toichi District}} {{s-aft|after=[[Shiki District}} {{end}} {{Kinai Five Provinces Districts}}
{{translated page|ja|十市郡|version=102374891|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 6]]
[[Category:History of Kashihara, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sakurai, Nara]]
}}
[[sn:Toichi district]] [[ja:十市郡]]

<!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--><!--Toichi district--> [[Category:Toichi District]] [[Category:Districts of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Former districts of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Sakurai, Nara]] [[Category:History of Kashihara, Nara]] [[Category:Municipal History of Tawaramoto]] [[Category:Shiki District]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:十市郡]] {{wikidata link|Q11405036}}

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:十市郡]][[zh:十市郡]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11405036]] Wikidata name: Toichi district [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:58, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Pathnavbox| *{{Pathnav|List of provinces of Japan|Kinai|Yamato Province}} *{{Pathnav|Japan|Kinki Region|Nara Prefecture}} }} [[File:Nara Toichi-gun.png|frame|The extent of Toichi District in Nara Prefecture]] '''Toichi District''' (十市郡, Tōichi-gun<ref>From ancient to medieval times, it was also read as "Tōchi-gun" (角川日本地名大辞典 29 奈良県).</ref>) was a [[:en:District|District]] in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] (formerly [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]).
== District Area ==
The area of the district when it was established as an administrative division in [[:en:1880|1880]] ([[Meiji]] 13) corresponds to the following regions:
* Parts of [[:en:Kashihara|Kashihara]], including Iitaka, Ōgaki, Toyoda, Niiguchi, Kamishōji, Uchizen, Kitayagi, Kihara, Yamanobō, Ishiharada, Deai, Kanzai, Shimohachitsuri, Kinomoto, and northeastern areas<ref>Exact details on [[address notation|residential address implementation]] in Yagi City remain unclear.</ref>. * Parts of [[:en:Sakurai|Sakurai]], including Arashiki, Higashishindō, Ōfuku, Kibi, Abe, Tani, Sakurai, Asako, Kurahashi, Kudario, and Awabara. * Parts of [[:en:Shiki District|Shiki District]], [[:en:Tawaramoto, Nara|Tawaramoto, Nara]], including Matsumoto, Nishitakeda, Jūroku-men, Hotsu, unnamed areas, Chiyo, and Aji-ma, southwest of these places.
== History ==
=== Ancient Period ===
==== Villages ====
The [[Wamyō Ruijushō]] lists the following villages in the district: * Iitomi * Kawabe (加八乃倍) * Ikegami * Kanbe
==== Shikinaisha (Ceremonial Shrines) ====
The [[Engishiki]] lists the following {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|qq=draft|lt=Shikinaisha}}: {{Shikinaisha header}} {{Shikinaisha list for Yamato Province}} {{Shikinaisha footer}}
=== Early Modern Period ===
According to the [[Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō]] (a record of early Meiji-era holdings), the following statuses were in place under the [[Tokugawa shogunate|shogunate]] administration and feudal lords. Lands managed directly by the [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] were under the jurisdiction of the [[Nara Bugyō]], with some containing [[temple lands]] (●) or [[exempt temple territories]] (○). There were three towns and 81 villages in total.
[Summary of holdings table and specific details provided.]
=== Modern Period ===
* [[:en:Keiō|Keiō]] 4 (1868): ** February 5 - [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]] territories transferred to the jurisdiction of the '''[[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]]'''. ** May 21 - Flag-holder territories and temple lands fall under Nara Prefecture. ** July 29 - Nara Prefecture renamed. * Meiji 4 (1871): ** July 14 - [[Abolition of the Han System]] leads to territorial divisions managed by [[Tsu Prefecture]], [[Kōriyama Prefecture]], [[Hisai Prefecture]], [[Tawaramoto Prefecture]], and [[Takatori Prefecture]]. ** November 22 - Administrative consolidation places the entire region under Nara Prefecture. * Meiji 13 (1880): ** April 15 - Toichi District is established under the [[Gun-ku-chōson System]]. * Meiji 30 (1897): ** April 1 - [[:en:Shiki District|Shiki District]], [[Toichi District]], and [[:en:Uda District|Uda District]] are merged to form the new [[:en:Shiki District|Shiki District]], leading to the abolition of Toichi District.
== Administration ==
; District Officials [Details of district officials across Nara, Osaka, and administrative transitions provided.]
== Notes == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=Japanese|editor="Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten" Editorial Committee|year=1990|title=[[Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten]]|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|volume=29 Nara Prefecture|isbn=4040012909}} * [https://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/up-cgi/login.pl?p=param/kyud/db_param Kyū-taka Kyū-ryō Torishirabechō Database] == See Also == * [[List of defunct districts of Japan]]
{{s-start}} {{s-bef|before=-----|title=Transition of Administrative Divisions|years= - 1897}} {{s-ttl|title=Toichi District}} {{s-aft|after=[[Shiki District}} {{end}} {{Kinai Five Provinces Districts}}
{{translated page|ja|十市郡|version=102374891|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:History of Kashihara, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sakurai, Nara]]
